D10 Imaging L5 Evaluate Reference

Sensor vs Eye: The Luther Mismatch

a camera sensor's red, green, blue are not a linear combination of the eye's — so two spectra the eye calls identical can record as different RGB

The Luther condition says a sensor reproduces colour exactly only when its R, G, B sensitivities are a linear combination of the human colour-matching functions. Real silicon sensors are not — their R, G, B curves are similar in shape but not exactly that. The consequence: two spectra that integrate to identical XYZ (look the same to the eye) can give different sensor RGB. A 3×3 colour-correction matrix patches the average case, never the corner cases.

Slide the second spectrum's secondary peak. Both spectra are kept eye-matched (close XYZ); watch the sensor RGB drift apart for the real sensor and stay locked together for a Luther-ideal one.
